items.title: Cloud adapter initialization exceptions not caught in resolveAdapter() — crashes on bad credentials
items.intent: A malformed stored credential produces a clear, actionable error message — never an uncaught exception stack trace.
items.description: src/cloud/provisioning/engine.ts lines 62-73 call createAwsAdapter() and createGcpAdapter() which can throw exceptions during token parsing (malformed JSON, missing fields). These calls are not wrapped in try-catch. If a user's stored token is malformed, the error propagates uncaught and crashes the provisioning flow without a helpful error message. DigitalOcean and Hetzner adapters do not have this issue — only AWS and GCP. Steps: (1) Read src/cloud/provisioning/engine.ts around lines 62-73 to see resolveAdapter() implementation. (2) Read src/cloud/provisioning/providers/aws.ts createAwsAdapter() and src/cloud/provisioning/providers/gcp.ts createGcpAdapter() to understand what exceptions they throw. (3) Wrap the adapter creation calls in try-catch in resolveAdapter(). (4) On catch, throw a descriptive error: 'Failed to initialize AWS/GCP adapter: credentials may be malformed. Re-run clawhq creds to reconfigure.'
steps: Read src/cloud/provisioning/engine.ts lines 62-73 — confirm createAwsAdapter() and createGcpAdapter() are called without try-catch
Read src/cloud/provisioning/providers/aws.ts createAwsAdapter() to identify what exceptions it throws and under what conditions
Read src/cloud/provisioning/providers/gcp.ts createGcpAdapter() to identify what exceptions it throws and under what conditions
Wrap createAwsAdapter() call in try-catch — on catch, throw new Error with message: 'Failed to initialize AWS adapter: credentials may be malformed. Re-run clawhq creds to reconfigure.'
Wrap createGcpAdapter() call in try-catch — on catch, throw new Error with message: 'Failed to initialize GCP adapter: service account JSON may be malformed. Re-run clawhq creds to reconfigure.'
Confirm DigitalOcean and Hetzner adapter initialization already handles errors gracefully
acceptanceCriteria: Calling resolveAdapter() with a malformed AWS token throws a descriptive Error, not an uncaught exception
Calling resolveAdapter() with malformed GCP service account JSON throws a descriptive Error, not an uncaught exception
Error messages reference 'clawhq creds' as the remediation path
DigitalOcean and Hetzner adapter init behavior is unchanged
items.groomingNotes: READY. engine.ts:62-73 confirmed calling createAwsAdapter() and createGcpAdapter() without try-catch via codebase inspection. Both adapters do token parsing that can throw. Fix is local to engine.ts — 2 try-catch blocks. No new deps. Scope: 1 file (engine.ts).

items.title: destroyInstance() leaves SSH private key on disk after instance is destroyed
items.intent: A destroyed instance must leave no credentials on disk — the SSH private key file must be deleted as part of the destroy flow, not left at ~/.clawhq/cloud/keys/<id>.pem forever.
items.description: src/cloud/provisioning/engine.ts destroyInstance() calls removeInstance() to clean the registry but never deletes the SSH private key file. The key is generated at src/cloud/provisioning/ssh-keygen.ts line 46 as deployDir/cloud/keys/<instanceId>.pem (mode 0600). After destroy, this file persists on disk indefinitely. The fix is in engine.ts destroyInstance(): after removeInstance() succeeds, read instance.sshKeyPath and delete the file if it exists. Use fs.rmSync with force:true so a missing file is not an error.
steps: Read src/cloud/provisioning/engine.ts destroyInstance() (lines ~258-295) — confirm removeInstance() is the last call and instance.sshKeyPath is accessible before removal
Read src/cloud/provisioning/ssh-keygen.ts — confirm the key file path pattern: deployDir/cloud/keys/<instanceId>.pem
In destroyInstance(), before calling removeInstance(): capture instance.sshKeyPath (it will be gone from registry after removal)
After removeInstance() returns true: if captured sshKeyPath is set, call fs.rmSync(sshKeyPath, { force: true }) to delete the private key file
Run src/cloud/provisioning tests: registry.test.ts and ssh-keygen.test.ts — confirm no regressions
acceptanceCriteria: After destroyInstance() succeeds, the SSH private key file at instance.sshKeyPath no longer exists on disk
If instance.sshKeyPath was not set (older registry entries), destroy completes without error
DigitalOcean, AWS, GCP, and Hetzner destroy flows are all covered — the fix is in engine.ts (shared path), not in provider adapters
items.groomingNotes: RE-GROOMED 2026-03-23. Elevated to must: verified destroy is a data sovereignty non-negotiable (CLAUDE.md). clawhq destroy must leave no credentials on disk — an SSH key persisting after destroy is a sovereignty violation, not just a cleanup miss. The intent field already uses must language. Security by default means cleanup is automatic. Fix is 3 lines in engine.ts — scope and steps unchanged.

items.title: SSH keys directory created without mode 0700 — key filenames world-listable on multi-user systems
items.intent: The SSH keys directory must not be listable by other users — mkdirSync must set mode 0700 so the directory contents are not exposed even though individual key files are mode 0600.
items.description: src/cloud/provisioning/ssh-keygen.ts line 37 calls mkdirSync(keysDir, { recursive: true }) without a mode option. This creates the keys directory (deployDir/keys/) with default permissions (0755 on most Linux systems), making the directory world-readable. While individual key files are mode 0600 (correct), any user on the system can ls the keys directory to enumerate which instance IDs have keys stored. The fix is to pass { recursive: true, mode: 0o700 } to mkdirSync, matching the same protection principle used for credentials.ts. The same issue exists in registry.ts line 57 for the registry parent directory, but that directory only contains the registry file which is already 0600.
steps: Read src/cloud/provisioning/ssh-keygen.ts lines 34-50 — confirm mkdirSync(keysDir, { recursive: true }) has no mode option
Change mkdirSync call to mkdirSync(keysDir, { recursive: true, mode: 0o700 }) — this ensures the directory is created owner-only even if an existing parent was 0755
Read src/cloud/provisioning/registry.ts writeInstanceRegistry() — confirm mkdirSync there also lacks a mode; add mode: 0o700 to that call as well
Run ssh-keygen.test.ts and registry.test.ts — confirm both pass and no regressions
acceptanceCriteria: The keys directory (deployDir/keys/) is created with mode 0700 — stat confirms no group/other read bits
The registry parent directory is created with mode 0700
Existing key file mode 0600 behavior is unchanged
items.groomingNotes: READY. ssh-keygen.ts:37 confirmed missing mode on mkdirSync. Two-file fix: ssh-keygen.ts (primary) + registry.ts (secondary, same pattern). No new deps. Scope: 2 files, 2 lines changed.
